chunk_summary: Feeding plans for non-breastfed children 6–24 months focus on infection prevention, micronutrient adequacy, and balanced feeding frequency with milk and complementary foods tailored to age-specific needs.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding frequency and composition for non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months.
Learn strategies to prevent iron deficiency and ensure adequate micronutrient intake.
Recognize the importance of hygiene and safe water in feeding plans.
Identify age-specific feeding adjustments and supplementation needs.
key_facts: Non-breastfed children are at higher risk of infection and micronutrient deficiencies.
From 6–11 months: 4–5 feedings/day (3 solid meals + 1–2 milk feeds); limit animal milk to ≤500 mL/day.
From 12–24 months: 3 family meals + 2 snacks; milk intake should be 250–500 mL/day.
Excess milk (>500 mL/day) can displace iron-rich foods and worsen anemia.
Use cups instead of bottles to reduce dental caries and contamination risks.
Monitor growth monthly for children <12 months or with suboptimal weight gain.
reference_quote: Non-breastfed infants and toddlers have higher vulnerability to infection and micronutrient gaps because they do not receive the anti-infective and nutrient contributions of breast milk. — Guiding principles for feeding non-breastfed children 6–24 months of age, WHO
document_key_topics: Feeding frequency and composition for non-breastfed children
Micronutrient supplementation and iron deficiency prevention
Hygiene and safe water in infant feeding plans
Growth monitoring and escalation for faltering weight
